text: So I'll be sharing today a robotic tap incisional flank hernia. This lady is a fifty-five year old female who came to me after having a sigmoid colectomy and had an extraction site that had developed a flank hernia that you see here on the CT scan. The defect measured about four by six centimeters and was right at the semilunar line. So I docked on her right lateral side. There were just a few little adhesions that I took down with cautery so that I could see the extent of the hernia. There was more momentum attached to that inferior portion of the hernia, but I decided to leave it in place knowing that I may have some difficulty getting a full preperitoneal flap down and may need to use that momentum for coverage.

The surgeon begins creating a flap with ports positioned just lateral of the midline. The right lower quadrant port is noted to be too close, causing some difficulty during dissection. The hernia appears longer and more medial than initially anticipated. The patient is positioned slightly in the left lateral decubitus with a towel under the abdomen. The surgeon carefully dissects the thin peritoneum, aiming to fully expose the hernia sac and achieve the 'volcano sign' before proceeding further.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum, revealing attenuated transversalis fascia. The camera is adjusted for better visibility as the surgeon identifies the thin hernia sacs. Dissection proceeds cephalad and caudad around the hernia, maintaining the preperitoneal plane. Retroperitoneal fat is easily separated from the transversalis fascia laterally. The hernia sac is visible, and a hole is made, prompting the surgeon to recruit more of the sac to facilitate placement of a non-coated mesh for full coverage.

The surgeon visualizes the lateral portion of the hernia and continues dissection in the preperitoneal plane, aiming to recruit as much of the hernia sac as possible to prevent seroma formation. Retroperitoneal fat is carefully dissected away, achieving a five-centimeter overlap on all sides. The anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) is palpable, allowing for precise dissection distal to the inferior border.

The surgeon struggles to dissect the caudal side due to the proximity of the right arm. The abdominal wall layers are visible, with the internal and external oblique muscles appearing bulky and together, while the transversalis fascia is separate. The surgeon plans to close these layers separately using a permanent OV lock suture, noting the ease of closure despite lower pneumoperitoneum pressure. Bites are taken from the pseudo hernia sac to prevent seroma formation.

The surgeon uses a six-inch permanent V-lock suture to close the transversalis layer, addressing the attenuated transversalis fascia visible near the camera. The true defect is closed first, followed by an evaluation to determine if additional sutures are needed for re-approximation.

The surgeon manipulates the transversalis muscle, causing it to bulge and bleed slightly. The camera angle is adjusted to provide a clearer view of the attenuated fascia. An absorbable OV lock suture is used to address the overlap at the attenuated portion, aiming for a four to five centimeter coverage beyond the medial edge.

The surgeon places a 15x15 cm circular macroporous Bard mesh over the hernia defect, aiming for a five-centimeter overlap. The mesh sits well within the surgical field, providing satisfactory coverage.

The surgeon measures the surgical space, ensuring it is approximately seven centimeters. The mesh is brought in and laid out flat, with a marked center aligned over the suture line to ensure proper fit without wrinkles.

The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture to secure the mesh at cardinal points, with one suture in the center and four around the perimeter to prevent sliding. The macroporous mesh adheres well to the anterior abdominal wall due to slight bleeding and dissection, despite lacking pro grip features.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture to close the flap, incorporating the omentum tacked to the inferior edge as additional tissue. The closure process involves handling visible holes and adjusting the flap without an additional robotic arm for support.

So I'm going to use the leftover, um, bike roll that I had to secure the mesh in. I'm going to close this hole with just a little purse string. Or, I'm sorry, a figure eight. And then I used another three-oh-like roll to close that hole there. And I was kind of really paranoid. I wanted to make sure that there wasn't a hole laterally that I couldn't appreciate because of that little mental flap. I didn't want anything to herniate and get stuck causing like an intervarietal hernia or have the mesh being exposed. But overall, I think the case went really well. Total time was about two and a half hours. She went home the same day. I have not yet seen her in follow up. But definitely a really interesting case. I just haven't seen a lot of spaghettians or I would say flank hernias where you can see the different layers of the abdominal wall so well and being able to kind of close both the anterior and posterior sheath I think gave me a really good repair. So thanks for watching. Feel free to critique and let me know what you think. Thanks.
